DIP: [{'DIP': 'The terminal phalanx of a digit, located at the distal end of the finger or toe, characterized by its small, compact, and dense bony structure with a slightly rounded or flattened distal surface. It is the most distal bone in the digit, articulating proximally with the middle phalanx at the distal interphalangeal joint. This bone is always isolated at the extremity of the digit, forming the terminal element without any associated skeletal components beyond its own anatomical boundaries. It is a single, discrete bone, distinct from proximal phalanges, metacarpals, metatarsals, and carpal bones, and is not part of a composite structure including multiple bones, joints, or surrounding soft tissue. The DIP is always the terminal bony element of the digit, with no additional skeletal structures extending beyond it.'}]
MCP: [{'MCP': 'A single, elongated cylindrical bone forming the proximal segment of a finger, characterized by a central shaft (diaphysis) and expanded ends (epiphyses). It is located at the base of the digit, articulating with the proximal phalanx at the metacarpal-phalangeal (MCP) joint and connecting to the carpal bones proximally. The bone is anatomically isolated, not part of a composite structure, and maintains consistent morphology across all digits. It functions as a key structural element for finger movement and support, and is distinctly identifiable as a standalone bone within the hand, separate from surrounding carpal bones and soft tissue.'}]
PIP: [{'PIP': 'The PIP (proximal interphalangeal) bones are the middle phalanges of the fingers, located exclusively within the digits between the proximal and distal phalanges. They are characterized by a uniform, elongated rectangular shape with consistent width along their length, minimal tapering, and a broad, flat surface. Positioned at the middle of each finger, they articulate with the proximal phalanx above and the distal phalanx below, forming a joint that allows flexion and extension. These bones are single, discrete elements within the phalangeal sequence, not found in the wrist or palm regions, and are distinct from the smaller, rounded terminal phalanges at the fingertip and the larger, more proximal metacarpal bones at the base of the hand. Their consistent rectangular morphology, uniform width, and central joint position are key visual identifiers, and they are not part of larger composite anatomical regions that include multiple bones or soft tissues. The PIP bones are specifically distinguished from the carpal bones in the wrist by their location within the fingers, their elongated rectangular shape, and their role in forming the interphalangeal joint, not by their proximity to the fingertip. They are also clearly differentiated from the metacarpal bones by their distal position, smaller size, and direct involvement in the finger joint structure, rather than serving as a connection between the wrist and the digits.'}]
Radius: [{'Radius': 'A single, compact, block-like bone located at the distal end of the forearm, forming part of the wrist joint. It is positioned laterally in the wrist, articulating with the carpal bones (specifically the scaphoid and lunate) and the base of the first metacarpal. The Radius exhibits a dense, solid structure with a trapezoidal or rectangular outline in anatomical views, distinguishing it from the smaller, irregularly shaped carpal bones. It is a distinct, isolated structure not connected to the terminal phalanges or the long bones of the forearm shaft, and is specifically the distal portion of the radius, not the elongated proximal shaft.'}]
Ulna: [{'Ulna': "A long, slender, tubular bone located in the medial forearm, extending from the elbow to the wrist. It is characterized by a continuous, linear structure with a relatively uniform diameter along its length, distinguishing it from the short, compact, and irregularly shaped carpal bones of the wrist. Positioned medially and aligned parallel to the radius, the ulna articulates proximally with the humerus and radius at the elbow and distally with the proximal row of carpal bones via the distal radioulnar joint. Its elongated, linear morphology and single, uninterrupted form contrast sharply with the clustered, multi-bone arrangement of the wrist. The ulna is identifiable by its length, tubular shape, and continuous form, which are distinct from the short, compact, and non-linear bones of the wrist. It is anatomically separated from the carpal bones by the distal radioulnar joint and is not part of the wrist joint. The ulna is not a short, compact bone and does not belong to the carpal row; it is exclusively a forearm bone with a distinct elongated structure, characterized by a smooth cortical outline and a central medullary cavity in radiographic imaging. It is located medially in the forearm, adjacent to the radius, and its distal end forms the ulnar side of the wrist joint, but it does not extend into the carpal row. The ulna is not a component of the wrist's carpal bones and is not part of the clustered, multi-bone arrangement of the wrist."}]
Wrist: [{'Wrist': 'A complex, articulated joint formed by the distal ends of the radius and ulna and the proximal row of carpal bones, creating a compact, multi-bone structure that serves as the primary pivot for hand movement. It is characterized by the interlocking arrangement of the eight carpal bones in two rows, with the proximal row articulating with the forearm bones and the distal row connecting to the metacarpals. The wrist is functionally and anatomically distinct from the forearm, as it does not include the long bones of the forearm (radius and ulna) beyond their distal articulations. Its structure enables a range of motion including flexion, extension, radial deviation, and ulnar deviation, and is defined solely by the carpal bone cluster and its immediate articulating surfaces with the forearm and hand.'}]
